Items.Title: 打造更好的会议 | Making Better Meetings
Items.Description:
我们都去过那里：一场本应是一封电子邮件或一条闲聊消息或什么都不是的会议。 没有什么比浪费时间更让我沮丧的了。 我以前说过，妈妈们是很棒的员工，因为我们没有时间可以浪费。 并非一天中的每一秒都必须“高效”，但当您的活动以提高效率为主要目的时，您最好确保它确实如此。

然而，在我们这里，我们中的许多人生活在一个远程工作的世界中，适应于降低生产力、积极性和增加挫败感的会议。 为什么我们让它发展到这个地步？ 为什么我们忽略了这样一个事实，即我们召开一堆糟糕的会议是在浪费公司的钱？ 因为这很容易。 因为计划会议应该与会议本身一样重要。 让我们停止召开糟糕的会议，并深入研究让我们的会议变得更好的方法。

这次会议应该是……不是会议

让我们将会议视为减速带。 当你把它们穿插在人们的生活中时，你就会减慢他们的势头。 如果每隔一小时开一次会，如果他们有大量时间从事项目、研究等工作，你就不能期望他们的工作效率与你应有的一样。

我认为值得注意的是，会议不应该是远程优先的公司对出现的问题的第一反应，尤其是当你与国际团队合作时。 我不知道我是否准备好说他们应该是最后的手段，但我接近做出这样的断言。

发送一条松散的消息或电子邮件而不是开会允许员工选择何时阅读消息并在日程安排中建立灵活性。

我们还应该认识到，会议不会发挥每个人的优势。 外向的人和外部处理者更有可能从会议中受益，而内向的人通常受益于有时间思考想法、处理并稍后返回讨论（书面或口头）。 如果你依赖于会议，你就没有考虑到你团队中的内向者。

规划

计划会议是什么意思？ 难道你不只是向你认为可能或应该感兴趣的每个人发出一些日历邀请吗？ 这是经常发生的事情，也是会议如此浪费的原因之一。 与正确拼写某人的电子邮件相比，计划会议应该花费时间、考虑和更多的努力。

谁需要在那里？

这不是垃圾蛋糕。 我们不需要所有涉及该主题的人都到场。 我一直喜欢计算每次会议的成本作为邀请所有人的威慑力的想法。 你可以通过计算那里每个人的小时工资并根据会议时间将其相加来做到这一点。 这并不意味着您应该取消所有会议或削减并销毁邀请列表。 这意味着您需要花时间确定谁应该在那里。 你是怎样做的？

从你的一长串人员名单开始，让我们通过回答以下问题来缩小范围：

  • 会议的目的是什么？
  • 谁就此主题做出决定？
  • 他们的意见是对话的重要组成部分吗？ 我想在这里指出，有对话输入，还有可以作为 slack 消息或电子邮件的一部分的输入。 并非每个人都必须参与对话。
  • 谁是主题专家？
  • 谁拥有可以从分享或聆听中获益的机构知识？

你不应该问自己“如果我不邀请他们，谁会不高兴？” 尽管这是思考过程中的一个常见部分，但不应影响邀请列表。 它应该影响的是清晰的沟通。 为什么你认为这个人的感情会受到伤害？ 您如何向他们传达这不是对他们的时间或他们工作的一部分的良好利用？

调度礼仪

  • 不要只是在没有上下文的情况下将会议放在某人的日历上。
  • 不要在未与受邀者联系的情况下移动或取消某人日历上的会议。
  • 提供议程。
  • 如果您希望事先完成工作，请将其放入邀请中。
  • 不要在某人的工作时间以外的时间将邀请放在某人的日历上。

会议礼仪

会前最佳实践

为了使会议最有效并充分利用人们的时间，重要的是：

  • 树立明确的目标。 例如，确定新活动的策略。
  • 定义会议和参与者的期望。 您是否希望参与者分享他们认为在发起活动之前需要完成的重要任务？ 你想讨论优点和缺点吗？
  • 询问参与者他们希望将什么作为议程的一部分。
  • 清楚地解释需要完成的任何前期工作以及当天的期望。

会议最佳实践

拜托，请不要以“我只是想我们可以聊聊 X”开始会议，然后茫然地盯着缩放屏幕。 制定议程就是制定会议大纲。 用它。 其他重要的事情：

  • 坚持议程，但不要死板到不允许任何其他讨论。
  • 尊重人们的时间。 如果你说会议将持续一个小时，那么它不应该在两个小时后结束。
  • 有一个议程执行者。 您可能会觉得会议范围有所扩大。 它发生了。 有时它是富有成效的。 大多数时候，它阻碍了召开富有成效的会议的能力。 通常，会议负责人应该是确保不会发生这种情况的议程执行者。 这不太符合今天的会议范围，但我们应该会后在闲暇时间谈一谈。”
  • 有一个时间管理员。 如果你想完成你议程上的每件事，你需要知道每件事需要多长时间。 这可能意味着限制讨论时间。 如果你安排了 5 分钟来谈论团队的优势，而最后四分钟只有一个人在谈论，那么你就没有按计划利用你的时间。 有一个人可以说，“James，让我们给其他人一些时间来分享”或“我们快要完成这个了。 让我们总结一下要点，然后确定这部分对话是否需要在之后进行。” 留出时间回答问题并在最后确定下一步。
  • 为每个人腾出空间。 通常房间里声音最大的人是那些有机会分享的人。 为确保每个人都有机会发表意见，请利用举手功能和聊天等支持功能，让人们以适合他们的方式参与。

召开一个好的会议很像写一篇好的论文。 你从一个目的开始——如果你是像我这样的英语书呆子，那就是一篇论文——然后你创建一个会议要点的大纲。 会议成为您填写大纲的过程的步骤。 你进行讨论，收集证据，提出你的论点，最后你应该有一个结论。 至少，您应该能够结束一章并进入下一章。 如果能很好地利用他们的时间，读者/参与者会在会议结束时感到满意。 他们将有动力继续下一章的工作。 他们会因完成工作和达到期望而受到赞赏。 如果您不确定自己是否达到目标，您所要做的就是寻求反馈。 如果您培养了诚实和分享的文化，这应该不是问题。 如果您还没有……好吧，那是另一篇博客文章。



原文:

We’ve all been there: a meeting that should’ve been an email or a slack message or really nothing at all. There are few things that frustrate me more than wasted time. I’ve said this before, moms are great employees because we don’t have any time to waste. Not every second of the day has to be “productive” but when you have an activity that has a primary purpose of being productive, you better make sure that it is.

And yet, here we are, many of us living in a remote-work world settling for meetings that decrease productivity, motivation, and increase frustration. Why have we let it get to this point? And why do we ignore the fact that we’re wasting company money by holding a bunch of bad meetings? Because it’s easy. Because planning a meeting should be just as important as the meeting itself. Let’s stop having bad meetings and dive into ways to make our meetings better.

This Meeting Should’ve Been…Not a Meeting

Let’s think of meetings as speedbumps. When you intersperse them throughout people’s days, you slow down their momentum. If there’s a meeting every other hour, you can’t expect the same productivity from your employee as you should if they have a solid block of time to work on projects, research, etc.

I think it’s worth noting that meetings should not be a remote-first company’s first response to a question or issue that comes up, especially if you’re working with an international team. I don’t know if I’m ready to say they should be a last resort, but I’m close to making that assertion.

Sending a slack message or an email rather than having a meeting allows employees to choose when the read the message and builds flexibility into the schedule.

We should also recognize that meetings do not utilize the strengths of everyone. Extroverts and external processors are more likely to benefit from meetings, whereas introverts often benefit from having time to think through ideas, process, and return to a discussion-written or verbal-later. If you’re relying on meetings, you’re not taking into consideration the introverts in your group.

Planning

What does it mean to plan a meeting? Don’t you just throw some calendar invites out to everyone that you think could or should be interested? This is what often happens and this is one of the reasons meetings are so wasteful. Planning meetings should take time, consideration, and more effort than spelling someone’s email correctly.

Who needs to be there?

This is not a dump-it cake. We don’t need everyone who touches the topic to be there. I’ve always loved the idea of calculating the cost of every meeting as a deterrent for inviting everyone. You can do that by calculating the hourly wage of everyone there and adding it up based on the time the meeting takes. That doesn’t mean that you should get rid of all meetings or slash and burn the invite list. It means that you need to take the time to determine who should be there. How do you do that?

Start with your long list of people and let’s narrow it down together by answering the following questions:

  • What’s the purpose of the meeting?
  • Who makes decisions about this topic?
  • Is their input an essential part of the conversation? I want to note here that there’s conversational input and there’s input that can come as part of a slack message or email. Not everyone has to be part of the conversation.
  • Who is a subject matter expert?
  • Who has institutional knowledge that would benefit from being there to share or to listen?

You should not ask yourself “who will get upset if I don’t invite them?” Although this is a common part of the thought process, it shouldn’t influence the invite list. What it should influence is clear communication. Why do you think this person’s feelings will be hurt? How can you communicate to them that it’s not a good use of their time or part of their job?

Scheduling Etiquette

  • Don’t just throw meetings on someone’s calendar without context.
  • Don’t move or cancel meetings on someone’s calendar without touching base with those invited.
  • Provide an agenda.
  • If you expect work to be done beforehand, put it in the invite.
  • Don’t put an invite on someone’s calendar for a time outside of their work hours.

Meeting Etiquette

Pre-meeting best practices

To make the meeting most efficient and a good use of people’s time, it’s important to:

  • Establish a clear purpose. For example, to finalize strategy for a new campaign.
  • Define expectations both of the meeting and of the participants. Do you want the participants to share what they think are important tasks that need to be completed before launching a campaign? Do you want to discuss strengths and weaknesses?
  • Ask the participants what they want to include as part of the agenda.
  • Clearly explain any pre-work that needs to be done as well as expectations for the day.

Meeting best practices

Please, please do not start a meeting with “I just thought we could chat about X” and then stare blankly at the zoom screen. What about X? Having an agenda is having an outline for a meeting. Use it. Other things that are important:

  • Stick to the agenda, but don’t be so rigid to not allow any other discussion.
  • Be respectful of people’s time. If you say the meeting is going to be an hour, it shouldn’t end after two hours.
  • Have an agenda enforcer. You might feel some scope creep in your meeting. It happens. Sometimes it’s productive. Most times it impedes the ability to have a productive meeting. Generally, the meeting leader should be the agenda enforcer who makes sure this doesn’t happen. “That’s a really great insight, Sam. This doesn’t quite fit into the scope of today’s meeting, but we should talk about this in slack after the meeting.”
  • Have a time keeper. If you want to accomplish everything on your agenda, you need to have a sense of how long everything will take. This might mean limiting discussion time. If you’ve scheduled 5 minutes to talk about the strengths of the team, and one person has talked for the last four minutes, you’re not utilizing your time as you planned. Having a person who can say, “James, let’s give someone else time to share” or “We’re getting close to time on this. Let’s summarize the main points and determine if this part of the conversation needs to happen in slack afterward.” Leave time to answer questions and determine next steps at the end.
  • Make space for everyone. Often the loudest people in the room are the ones who have the opportunity to share. To ensure everyone has an opportunity to be heard, utilize support features like the handraise function and the chat to allow people to participate in ways that work for them.

Running a good meeting is a lot like writing a good paper. You start with a purpose-a thesis if you’re a English nerd like me-and then you create an outline of the main points of the meeting. The meeting becomes the step of the process where you fill in the outline. You have the discussion, collect the evidence, make your arguments, and by the end you should have a conclusion. At the very least, you should be able to end a chapter that will lead into the next one. The reader/participant will feel satisfied at the end of the meeting if it’s a good use of their time. They’ll be motivated to work on the next chapter. They’ll feel appreciated for accomplishing their work and meeting expectations. And if you’re not sure if you’ve hit the mark, all you have to do is ask for feedback. If you’ve cultivated a culture of honesty and sharing, this shouldn’t be a problem. If you haven’t…well, that’s a different blog post.
Items.Author: bekahhw

Items.Title: 2021/2022 年 DEV 书签/阅读清单摘录 | DEV Bookmarks / Reading List Excerpts from 2021/2022
Items.Description:
英戈史坦克

发表于一些 DEV 帖子的注释集合，包括我自己和其他人的工作，仅部分按顺序排列，作为定期更新的粘性帖子。

我创建这个列表是为了回顾我最近的开发活动、书签和讨论，所以有很多 2022 年和 2021 年的内容。我想在标题中加上“2023”，作为点击诱饵预告片和更新提醒这篇文章超过 2022 年。

由于一些主题可能不如其他主题那么好，一些讨论可能很快就会过时，尤其是在涉及 Web 开发教程和关于新的和即将推出的 CSS 和 JavaScript 功能的讨论时，我不打算更新这篇文章2023 年，所以我将至少在 2024 年跟进另一个列表。

我还要感谢@grahamthedev的讨论、提示和鼓励，感谢@ben和@devteam 在社交媒体上展示我的内容，以及其他所有人的贡献和参与！

目录

关于开发、社交媒体、网络、写作和内容创建的讨论

咆哮和模仿帖子

关于编码、网页设计和（Web）开发的各个方面

其他网络开发文章

使用 Linux 和其他开发人员资源

更多我自己的开发内容

我的 DEV 阅读列表中的其他摘录

聚会和联合办公：在哪里可以与德国的开发人员联系

  • 在柏林结识酷人的最佳咖啡馆
  • 虚拟和实际事件 2022

在 DEV.to 上哪里可以找到您自己的阅读清单

一旦暂停，ingosteinke 将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

解除暂停后，ingosteinke 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，ingosteinke 的所有帖子都将隐藏起来，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 ingosteinke 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

感谢您保护 DEV 社区 👩‍💻👨‍💻 的安全。 您可以通过以下方式标记 ingosteinke：

报告其他不当行为

取消标记 ingosteinke 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Ingo Steinke

Posted on Nov 22

An annotated collection of some DEV posts, including my own and other people's work, only partially in order, as a sticky post to be updated regularly.

I created this list looking back on my recent DEV activity, bookmarks, and discussions, so there is a lot of content from 2022 and 2021. I thought about putting "2023" in the title, as a clickbait teaser and as a reminder to update this post beyond 2022.

As some of the topics might not age as good as others, and some discussions might be obsolete soon, especially when it comes to web development tutorials and discussions about new and upcoming CSS and JavaScript features, I don't plan to update this post beyond 2023, so I shall follow up with another list at least in 2024.

I also want to thank @grahamthedev for discussion, tips, and encouragement, @ben and the @devteam for featuring my content on social media, and everyone else for their contribution and engagement!

Table of Contents

(TODO add anchor links to document sections)

Discussion about DEV, Social Media, Networking, Writing, and Content Creation

Rants and Parody Posts

About Coding, Web Design, and various Aspects of (Web) Development

CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) and HTML Development

JavaScript and TypeScript Development

Other Web Development Articles

WordPress and WoCommerce Development

Using Linux and other Developer Resources

Some more of my own DEV Content

Other Excerpts from my DEV Reading List

Meetups and Coworking: where to connect with fellow devs in Germany

  • Best cafés to meet cool people in Berlin
  • Virtual and actual events 2022

Where to find your own Reading List on DEV.to

dev.to/readinglist is a link to my full reading list, but beware, it will take everyone to their own DEV reading list instead.

Michael Tharrington (he/him) - Nov 7

Once suspended, ingosteinke will not be able to comment or publish posts until their suspension is removed.

Once unsuspended, ingosteinke will be able to comment and publish posts again.

Once unpublished, all posts by ingosteinke will become hidden and only accessible to themselves.

If ingosteinke is not suspended, they can still re-publish their posts from their dashboard.

Thanks for keeping DEV Community 👩‍💻👨‍💻 safe. Here is what you can do to flag ingosteinke:

ingosteinke consistently posts content that violates DEV Community 👩‍💻👨‍💻's code of conduct because it is harassing, offensive or spammy.

Report other inappropriate conduct

Unflagging ingosteinke will restore default visibility to their posts.
Items.Author: ingosteinke

Items.Title: #gitPanic - 文件 | #gitPanic - Files
Items.Description:
我之所以了解这些命令，是因为我抱怨 git 不能可靠地跟踪我在GUI （Finder、VS Code）中所做的文件更改。 事实证明，git 将始终跟踪文件名更改、移动文件和删除文件……如果你告诉它这些。

本博客假设您对 git 和命令行有基本的了解，或者已经阅读了git 101和HEAD 。

  1. 提交
  2. 添加
  3. 提交

    如果您不想使用 git 的命令来删除或修改文件，只需告诉它在您使用git commit -a或git commit --all提交时暂存这些更改。 您仍然需要添加 git 之前未跟踪过的新文件。

    添加

    使用git add . 将添加新文件和删除的文件，但不会暂存文件名更改。 您可以在使用git add -u或git add --update提交之前暂存对所有跟踪文件的所有更改。 您可以使用git add -A匹配当前工作目录所需的所有更改，包括新文件。 再一次，A 代表所有。 您还可以在此选项后传递文件路径以仅包含该文件的所有更改。

    跑步

    工作方式类似于sudo rm -rf ，但 git 会跟踪更改。

    使用git mv告诉 git 在您更新文件路径时跟踪更改。 你可以用它来

    重命名文件

    git mv <oldName> <newName>

    并移动一个文件

    git mv <fileName> <newDirectory>

    与ls一样， git ls-files将列出目录中的所有文件。 不同之处在于git ls-files考虑了远程存储库、本地存储库、索引和工作目录。 例如，跑步

    将向您显示与上次提交不同的所有暂存文件。 这仅仅是开始。 还有更多选项可以向您显示有关您的回购的各种信息。

    与git ls-files类似， git ls-tree将列出存储库中的所有数据类型。 同样，有许多格式化选项。

    您必须将工作目录传递给 ls-tree 之类的

    在这种情况下，ref 必须是树状对象（又名目录），如 HEAD 或分支名称。 您还可以在树状对象中指定文件路径，例如

    结论

    这些命令真正显示了 git 存储了多少关于你的 repo 和你所做的更改的信息！



原文:

I only learned about these commands because I was complaining about git not reliably tracking changes to files I made in the GUI (Finder, VS Code). Turns out git will always track file name changes, moving files, and deleting files ...if you tell it about them.

This blog assumes you have a basic understanding of git and command line or have read git 101 and HEAD.

  1. git commit
  2. git add
  3. git rm
  4. git mv
  5. git ls-files
  6. git ls-tree

git commit

If you don't want to use git's commands to delete or modify files, just tell it to stage those changes when you commit using git commit -a or git commit --all. You'll still have to add new files that git hasn't tracked before.

git add

Using git add . will add new files and deleted files, but won't stage file name changes. You can stage all changes to all tracked files before committing using git add -u or git add --update. You can stage all changes required to match your current working directory, including new files, with git add -A. Once again, A stands for all. You can also pass a file path after this option to only include all changes for that file.

git rm

Running

works like sudo rm -rf, but git tracks the changes.

git mv

Using git mv tells git to track the changes while you update the file path. You can use it to

rename a file

git mv <oldName> <newName>

and move a file

git mv <fileName> <newDirectory>

git ls-files

Like ls, git ls-files will list all the files in your directory. The difference is git ls-files takes into account the remote repository, local repository, index, and working directory. For example, running

will show you all the staged files that are different from the last commit. This is just the beginning. There are many more options that will show you all kinds of information about your repo.

git ls-tree

Similar to git ls-files, git ls-tree will list all of the data types in your repository. There are, again, many options for formatting.

You have to pass a working directory to ls-tree like

In this case, the ref has to be to a tree-ish object (aka directory) like HEAD or a branch name. You can also specify a file path in a tree-ish object like

Conclusion

These commands really show just how much information git is storing about your repo and the changes you make!
